# Part 8 — Designing Circuits {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## The Engineering Design Cycle

Engineering isn't just "plugging things in"—it's a deliberate, repeatable cycle of prediction and observation:

::: {.incremental}
1. **Define the Goal** — *What should happen? (e.g., "An LED turns on when dark")*
2. **Select Components** — *What sensors and actuators do we need?*
3. **Draft the Schematic** — *How are they connected logically?*
4. **Calculate Values** — *What resistor sizes are safe? (Ohm's Law!)*
5. **Simulate (Optional)** — *Test the logic in Falstad or Wokwi.*
6. **Prototype** — *Build the physical circuit on a Breadboard.*
7. **Test & Iterate** — *Observe results, fix errors, and optimize.*
:::

## The Language of Design: Schematics

If we want to build a house, we look at a **blueprint**. In electronics, that's a **Schematic**. It shows the *logic* of the connections.

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="45%"}
### Why use symbols?
- **Universal**: An engineer in Tokyo and London can read the same drawing.
- **Clear**: No messy wires or overlapping photos.
- **Fast**: Symbols are optimized for quick drafting.
::::

:::: {.column width="55%"}

| Component | Schematic Symbol |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Power (+V)**  | ![](assets/battery_sym.svg){width="35px"} (or +5V label) |
| **Ground (GND)** | ![](assets/gnd_sym.svg){width="35px"} (The Drain) |
| **Resistor**    | ![](assets/resistor_sym.svg){width="40px"} |
| **LED**         | ![](assets/led_sym.svg){width="40px"} |
| **Switch**      | ![](assets/switch_sym.svg){width="40px"} |
| **Capacitor**   | `—││—` (Filter/Tank) |

::::

:::::

## Reading a Schematic: The Flow

To read a circuit like a pro, think of it as a **Gravity System**:

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="60%"}
### 1. The Vertical Flow $\downarrow$
- **High Pressure (+V)** is at the **TOP**.
- **The Drain (GND)** is at the **BOTTOM**.
- *Current always flows "downhill" from Power to Ground.*

### 2. The Horizontal Logic $\rightarrow$
- **Inputs** (Sensors/Switches) on the **Left**.
- **Outputs** (LEDs/Motors) on the **Right**.
- *Read connections like a book: Left to Right.*
::::

:::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.callout-note}
## Pro Tip
If a schematic is drawn poorly (GND on top, Power on bottom), it's much harder to "see" the electricity moving. Always stick to the **Downhill Rule**.
:::
::::

:::::

## KVL: Conservation of Energy

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="55%"}
**The Principle:** Every volt added by the supply must be *dropped* (used up) somewhere in the loop. You cannot return to the battery with "leftover" pressure.

$$\text{Voltage}_{\text{Supply}} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 \dots$$

::: {.callout-note .fragment}
## The Roller Coaster Analogy
The chain lift pulls you up to the peak (+V). Every drop and loop after that uses up that height. You must be at 0 height to return to the start.
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}
![](assets/water_analogy_led.png){width="100%"}
::::

:::::

## Interactive: Voltage Loops

<iframe src="../simulations/kirchhoff/index.html?tab=kvl"
  style="width:100%;height:450px;border:none;border-radius:12px;"
  allowfullscreen>
</iframe>

**Observation:** The Battery adds 5V. Notice how each resistor "drops" (uses up) some of it. 
*Does the sum of $V_1 + V_2$ ever change, even if you change the resistance?*


## KVL in Practice: The "Leftover" Voltage

Why do we need a resistor for a simple LED? 

::: {.incremental}
1. **The Supply**: Your battery provides **5V**.
2. **The LED**: A standard Red LED only needs **2V**.
3. **The Problem**: Without a resistor, the extra **3V** of pressure will force too much current into the LED—making it go **POP**.
4. **The Solution**: A resistor "absorbs" that extra 3V, keeping the LED safe. 
:::

## KCL: Conservation of Charge

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="55%"}
**The Principle:** Whatever current flows *into* a junction (node) must flow *out* of it. Electricity isn't a gas; it can't "compress" or pile up.

$$\text{Current}_{\text{In}} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 \dots$$

::: {.callout-note .fragment}
## The Highway Analogy
Think of a highway interchange. 100 cars enter. If 40 exit West and 60 exit East, all 100 are accounted for. No cars vanish.
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}
![](assets/water_divider_closed_loop.png){width="100%"}
::::

:::::

## Interactive: Current Branches

<iframe src="../simulations/kirchhoff/index.html?tab=kcl"
  style="width:100%;height:450px;border:none;border-radius:12px;"
  allowfullscreen>
</iframe>

**Observation:** Watching the "moving charges" (dots). At the junction, the flow splits into two paths.
*Does any charge disappear at the junction? Is the total flow in equal to the total flow out?*


## KCL in Practice: Parallel Paths

In robotics, we often have many components (motors, sensors, LEDs) sharing the same battery.

::: {.incremental}
- **The Junction**: The battery's positive rail acts as a massive junction.
- **The Split**: Current flows out of the battery and **splits** into each component path.
- **The Sum**: Every milliamp drawn by a motor or LED is added together. This is why high-power robots need thick wires—to carry the *sum* of all those currents!
:::

## Design Exercise: Safe LED — KVL in Action {background-color="#00C9A7" .sdaia-dark}

*(design)* Target: Limit current to **20 mA** using a **3.3 V** supply.
*(Assume LED voltage drop = 2 V)*

::: {.fragment}
### Step 1: Apply KVL — Find the Resistor Voltage
$V_{resistor} = V_{supply} - V_{led} = 3.3V - 2.0V = 1.3V$
*KVL says: all voltage must be used up — the resistor absorbs the leftover 1.3 V.*

### Step 2: Calculate Resistance
$R = V / I = 1.3V / 0.02A = 65 \Omega$

### Step 3: Select Standard Value
Nearest standard: **68 \Omega**. 
*Always round UP for safety. 100 \Omega is also common for "dimmer but safer" LEDs.*
:::

## DANGER: The Short Circuit {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="radial-gradient(circle at 70% 30%, #450a0a 0%, #020617 100%)"}

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="65%"}

A **Short Circuit** happens when you connect **5V directly to GND** with a bare wire. 

::: {.fragment}
- **Resistance ≈ 0 \Omega ** (no obstacle)
- **Flow (Current) ≈ $\infty$** (dangerous speed!)
:::

::: {.fragment}
This generates intense heat, can trigger a "burning" smell, and will **fry your Arduino's chip** or your laptop's USB port instantly.
:::

::: {.callout-important .fragment}
## Rule #1: The Safety Buffer
**Never** connect 5V to GND without a "buffer" component (like a resistor or LED) to regulate the flow.
:::

::::

:::: {.column width="35%"}

![](assets/short_circuit_danger_icon.png){width="100%"}

::::

:::::

## Design Heuristics (Pro Tips)

When you're designing a quick prototype, start with these "Goldilocks" values:

| Use Case | Standard Value | Color Code | Why? |
|----------|----------------|------------|------|
| **Power LED (5V)** | **220 \Omega - 330 \Omega** | Red-Red-Brown | Safe & Bright. |
| **Signal Pull-up** | **10 k\Omega** | Brown-Black-Orange | Stable "Listening". |
| **Precision Setup**| **1 k\Omega** | Brown-Black-Red | Fast response. |

::: {.callout-tip}
## The "Finger Test"
If you build a circuit and a component feels hot to the touch—**UNPLUG IT**. Something is wired incorrectly or a resistor is too small.
:::

## Meet the Breadboard

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="60%"}

A breadboard lets you build circuits **without soldering**. 

Think of it as a **temporary LEGO board** for electrons.

::: {.fragment .highlight-blue}
- **Terminal rows** (A–E / F–J) — each row of 5 holes is **one solid wire**.
:::

::: {.fragment .highlight-red}
- **Power rails** (+ and −) — run vertically the full length of the board.
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Centre divider** — a gap that separates the two halves; designed to straddle chips.
:::

::::

:::: {.column width="40%"}

![](assets/breadboard_400_standard.png){width="100%"}

::: {.callout-note}
## The 1-Second Rule
Same row = Connected. Different row = Separated.
:::

::::

:::::

## The "X-Ray" View

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="50%"}

If you peeled off the plastic, you'd see **metal clips** inside.

::: {.fragment}
- Each clip grabs onto the legs of your components.
- The 5 holes in a row (e.g. A10, B10, C10, D10, E10) are all **connected by one piece of metal**.
:::

::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}

![](assets/breadboard_400_xray.png){width="100%"}

::::

:::::

## Spot the Error! {background-color="#00C9A7" .sdaia-dark}

Which connection attempt is **Wrong** and why?

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="33%"}
### A
![](assets/breadboard_led_correct.png){width="100%"}
*(LED legs in Row 5 and Row 6)*
::::

:::: {.column width="33%"}
### B
![](assets/breadboard_resistor_shorted.png){width="100%"}
*(Resistor legs both in Row 10)*
::::

:::: {.column width="33%"}
### C
![](assets/breadboard_button_straddle.png){width="100%"}
*(Button straddling the centre divider)*
::::

:::::

::: {.fragment}
**Answer:** **B is wrong.** Since both legs are in the same metal clip (Row 10), the electricity takes the shortcut through the clip and ignores the resistor!
:::

## Powering the Board: The Rails

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="55%"}

To give your circuit life, you need **Pressure (V)** and a **Drain (GND)**.

- **Red Rail (+)**: Connect to **5V** or **3.3V**.
- **Blue Rail (−)**: Connect to **GND** (The Ground).

::: {.callout-note}
## Relative Ground
All voltages are measured *relative to GND* (0V). 
Think of it as the **water level in the drain** — everything flows toward it.
:::

::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}

![](assets/breadboard_400_standard.png){width="100%"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pro tip
Wire the Rails first! Then you can pull power for your components from anywhere on the long side strips.
:::

::::

:::::
